perturbation.original_text: Confidential information (“Information”) means and includes, but is not limited to, the additional confidential and proprietary intell ectual property regard ing existing installations that is provided by the State Bar after submission of this agreemen t.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential information (“Information”) means and includes only that information explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in writing by the State Bar after submission of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition is broad, encompassing various forms of confidential information. The modified text narrows it significantly, requiring explicit written designation. This contradicts standard interpretations of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The modified clause requires explicit marking, which is not a prerequisite under CUTSA. Reasonable efforts, not just explicit marking, satisfy the law. The modified text could allow the disclosure of trade secrets that were subject to other methods of protection but not explicitly marked, which would remove legal protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by requiring explicit written designation ('Confidential' in writing), narrows the scope of confidential information significantly, deviating from the broader protection offered by CUTSA, which focuses on 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. The original text's broader definition aligns more closely with protecting information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known, as intended by trade secret law. The provided snippet, 'California Code, CIV 3426...This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act,' indicates that California adheres to the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which emphasizes reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, not solely explicit marking. Therefore, the change introduces a contradiction by potentially allowing the disclosure of trade secrets protected by other methods if they are not explicitly marked.

perturbation.original_text: Company agrees that it will reta in all Information provided in connection with the RFP in the strictest confidence, and will neither use nor di sclose such Information to anyone other than those who, in each case, need to know the Information for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees that it will retain all Information provided in connection with the RFP with reasonable confidence, and may disclose such Information to anyone who, in their judgment, may benefit from it.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates 'strictest confidence' and limits disclosure to a 'need to know' basis. The modified text lowers the standard to 'reasonable confidence' and allows for disclosure to anyone who may 'benefit' from the information, directly contradicting the confidentiality requirement and creating uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute addresses interpreting contracts according to the ordinary meaning of the words, or special meaning if the context suggests. 'Strictest confidence' has a far stronger ordinary meaning than 'reasonable confidence,' and 'need to know' is far more restrictive than 'may benefit.' The modification effectively changes the contractual obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1644. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1644. The words of a contract are to be understood in their ordinary and popular sense, rather than according to their strict legal meaning; unless used by the parties in a technical sense, or unless a special meaning is given to them by usage, in which case the latter must be followed. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent and creates a legal contradiction by weakening the confidentiality obligations. The original text requires 'strictest confidence' and disclosure only on a 'need to know' basis, while the modified text lowers this to 'reasonable confidence' and allows disclosure to anyone who 'may benefit.' This deviation contradicts the principle in California Code, CIV 1644, which states that contract words should be understood in their ordinary sense. 'Strictest confidence' and 'need to know' have much stronger ordinary meanings than 'reasonable confidence' and 'may benefit,' respectively, leading to a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges and agrees that the reme dy at law for any breach of this NDA is inadequate and that, in additi on to monetary damages, including but not limited to special, incidental, consequential or puniti ve damages, and any other avai lable relief at law, whether based in contract, tort or otherwise, the State Bar will be entitled to specific performance, injunctive relief or any other eq uitable remedy without the need to (i) prove actual damages and/or (ii) post any bond or other security deposit in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges and agrees that the remedy at law for any breach of this NDA is adequate and that the State Bar will only be entitled to monetary damages. The State Bar must prove actual damages and post a bond or other security deposit in connection with such remedy.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides for equitable remedies like specific performance and injunctive relief and waives the need to prove actual damages or post a bond. The modified text eliminates equitable remedies, requires proof of damages, and mandates a bond, directly contradicting the original terms and standard remedies for breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: This code outlines situations where an injunction may be granted. While the original contract acknowledges that a breach of NDA would cause irreparable harm and justifies an injunction, the modified contract removes this provision, potentially preventing the State Bar from obtaining an injunction even if the criteria of section 526 are met.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 526 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The changed text fundamentally contradicts the original agreement regarding remedies for a breach of the NDA. The original text allows for equitable remedies (specific performance, injunctive relief) and waives the need to prove actual damages or post a bond. The changed text eliminates these equitable remedies, requires proof of actual damages, and mandates posting a bond. This contradicts the original agreement and undermines the potential for injunctive relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1, CCP 526(a), outlines conditions under which an injunction may be granted, including when pecuniary compensation is inadequate or difficult to ascertain. By removing the possibility of injunctive relief and requiring proof of actual damages, the modified contract could prevent the State Bar from obtaining an injunction, even if the criteria of section 526 are met, therefore contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, this NDA will no t prohibit Company from disclosing any Information if required by an appropriate legal or regulatory author ity, provided that, prior to any disclosure: (iii) Company provides the State Bar with prompt written notice of the required disclosure so that the State Bar may seek such protective order as it may deem appropriate; and (iv) disclosure of any Information is limited only to the extent legally required and Company uses its best efforts to obtain assurances that confidential treatment will be accorded such disclosed Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, this NDA will not prohibit Company from disclosing any Information if required by any authority. Company is not required to inform the State Bar prior to disclosure, and disclosure is not limited.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows disclosure only when 'required by an appropriate legal or regulatory authority,' mandates notice to the State Bar, and limits disclosure to the extent legally required. The modified text removes all these safeguards, permitting disclosure to 'any authority' without notice or limitations, effectively negating the confidentiality provisions. This is a significant departure that could make the NDA unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This protects trade secrets. The original clause sought to balance disclosure requirements with protecting information covered by that law. The modified clause permits any disclosure to any 'authority' without the original safeguards, destroying any potential trade secret protection if the company decided to disclose it. The modifications will create uncertainty as to whether the trade secret is still protected.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 1060 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 8. PRIVILEGES [900 - 1070] ( Division 8 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 4. Particular Privileges [930 - 1063] ( Chapter 4 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) ARTICLE 11. Trade Secret [1060 - 1063] ( Article 11 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) 1060. If he or his agent or employee claims the privilege, the owner of a trade secret has a privilege to refuse to disclose the secret, and to prevent another from disclosing it, if the allowance of the privilege will not tend to conceal fraud or otherwise work injustice. (Enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original text because it removes key safeguards related to disclosing confidential information. The original NDA allows disclosure only when mandated by an 'appropriate legal or regulatory authority,' requires notice to the State Bar, and limits the scope of disclosure. The altered version permits disclosure to 'any authority' without these limitations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, EVID 1060, states that the owner of a trade secret has a privilege to refuse to disclose the secret. By removing the requirement to disclose only to an 'appropriate' authority and eliminating limitations on disclosure, the modified text weakens the protection of trade secrets and confidentiality, making it easier to disclose the secret, undermining the privilege to refuse disclosure.
